revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
[[Category:Mahayana sutras]] [[Category:Yogacara]] [[Category:Vaipulya sutras]]

revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]

==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>

{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>

{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.

==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].

revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}

revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}

revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]

{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]

{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}

The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>

{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>

revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]] ]]
]]
]]
]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]] ]]

==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].

[[sn:Ten Stages Sutra]] [[en:Ten Stages Sutra]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[bn:দশভূমিক সূত্র]] {{Commons category|Daśabhūmika Sūtra}} [[et:Kümne taseme suutra]] [[fr:Dashabhumika sutra]] [[hi:दशभूमीश्वर]] [[ja:十地経]] [[no:Daśabhūmikasūtraen]] [[sv:Daśabhūmika-sūtra]] [[ta:தசபூமிக சூத்திரம்]] [[vi:Thập địa kinh]] [[zh:十地經]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Pages and Categories from Wikipedia
revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[Mahayana]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[Avatamsaka Sutra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]] ]]

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[Huayan school]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[:en:Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[:en:Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[:en:Buddha-nature|Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[:en:Bodhiruci|Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]] ]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove incorrect Shona (sn:) interwiki links
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3234449]] Wikidata name: Ten Stages Sutra [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[:en:Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[:en:Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[:en:Buddha-nature|Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[:en:Bodhiruci|Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Mahayana sutras]]
[[Category:Vaipulya sutras]]
}}
[[en:Ten Stages Sutra]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]

Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[:en:Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[:en:Buddha-nature|Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[:en:Bodhiruci|Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দশভূমিক সূত্র]][[sn:commons:Category:Daśabhūmika Sūtra]][[en:Ten Stages Sutra]][[et:Kümne taseme suutra]][[fr:Dashabhumika sutra]][[hi:दशभूमीश्वर]][[ja:十地経]][[no:Daśabhūmikasūtraen]][[sv:Daśabhūmika-sūtra]][[ta:தசபூமிக சூத்திரம்]][[vi:Thập địa kinh]][[zh:十地經]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3234449]] Wikidata name: Ten Stages Sutra [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ([[:en:Sanskrit|Sanskrit]]: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[:en:Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[:en:Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[:en:Buddha-nature|Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[:en:Bodhiruci|Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]

The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[:en:Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.

==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[:en:Bodhiruci|Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.

The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]

The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[:en:Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.

The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].

==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]]

The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''[[:en:Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}]]''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes [[:en:Bhūmi (Buddhism)|ten stages of development]] that a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of [[:en:Buddha-nature|Buddha-nature]] and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by [[:en:Vasubandhu|Vasubandhu]] in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by [[:en:Bodhiruci|Bodhiruci]] and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A [[:en:Dashabhumika|Daśabhūmikā]] school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the [[:en:Huayan|Huayan]], as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as [[:en:Zen|Zen]].
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]]

The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''{{ill|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|en|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|WD=Q790258|lt={{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}}}''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes {{ill|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|en|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|WD=Q2517942|lt=ten stages of development}} that a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva}} must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of {{ill|Buddha-nature|en|Buddha-nature|WD=Q640962|lt=Buddha-nature}} and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu}} in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by {{ill|Bodhiruci|en|Bodhiruci|WD=Q2324914|lt=Bodhiruci}} and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A {{ill|Dashabhumika|en|Dashabhumika|WD=Q155237|lt=Daśabhūmikā}} school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the {{ill|Huayan|en|Huayan|WD=Q1198075|lt=Huayan}}, as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}.
==See also== * {{ill|Chinese Buddhism|en|Chinese Buddhism|WD=Q847618|lt=Chinese Buddhism}}
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]]

Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu}} in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by {{ill|Bodhiruci|en|Bodhiruci|WD=Q2324914|lt=Bodhiruci}} and others during the 6th century CE.

The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. ==See also== * {{ill|Chinese Buddhism|en|Chinese Buddhism|WD=Q847618|lt=Chinese Buddhism}}
==Notes== <references/>

{{Buddhism topics}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দশভূমিক সূত্র]][[co:commons:Category:Daśabhūmika Sūtra]][[en:Ten Stages Sutra]][[et:Kümne taseme suutra]][[fr:Dashabhumika sutra]][[hi:दशभूमीश्वर]][[ja:十地経]][[no:Daśabhūmikasūtraen]][[sv:Daśabhūmika-sūtra]][[ta:தசபூமிக சூத்திரம்]][[vi:Thập địa kinh]][[zh:十地經]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দশভূমিক সূত্র]][[co:commons:Category:Daśabhūmika Sūtra]][[en:Ten Stages Sutra]][[et:Kümne taseme suutra]][[fr:Dashabhumika sutra]][[hi:दशभूमीश्वर]][[ja:十地経]][[no:Daśabhūmikasūtraen]][[sv:Daśabhūmika-sūtra]][[ta:தசபூமிக சூத்திரம்]][[vi:Thập địa kinh]][[zh:十地經]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দশভূমিক সূত্র]][[sn:commons:Category:Daśabhūmika Sūtra]][[en:Ten Stages Sutra]][[et:Kümne taseme suutra]][[fr:Dashabhumika sutra]][[hi:दशभूमीश्वर]][[ja:十地経]][[no:Daśabhūmikasūtraen]][[sv:Daśabhūmika-sūtra]][[ta:தசபூமிக சூத்திரம்]][[vi:Thập địa kinh]][[zh:十地經]]
{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit}}: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''{{ill|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|en|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|WD=Q790258|lt={{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}}}''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes {{ill|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|en|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|WD=Q2517942|lt=ten stages of development}} that a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva}} must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of {{ill|Buddha-nature|en|Buddha-nature|WD=Q640962|lt=Buddha-nature}} and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu}} in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by {{ill|Bodhiruci|en|Bodhiruci|WD=Q2324914|lt=Bodhiruci}} and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A {{ill|Dashabhumika|en|Dashabhumika|WD=Q155237|lt=Daśabhūmikā}} school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the {{ill|Huayan|en|Huayan|WD=Q1198075|lt=Huayan}}, as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}.
==See also== * {{ill|Chinese Buddhism|en|Chinese Buddhism|WD=Q847618|lt=Chinese Buddhism}}
==Notes== <references/>
== External links == * [http://www.buddhanet.net/e-learning/history/s_mahasutras.htm List of Mahayana Sutras] * [http://www.thebuddhadharma.com/issues/2006/fall/phenomenal.html The Phenomenal Flower Garland] * {{cite book | author = Yin-Shun | title = The Way to Buddhahood: Instructions from a Modern Chinese Master | publisher = Wisdom Publications | year = 1998 | isbn = 0-86171-133-5 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/waytobuddhahood00yins }} * [https://read.84000.co/translation/toh44-31.html An English translation by 84000: Translating the Words of the Buddha]
{{Buddhism topics}}
‎]]]]

They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes {{ill|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|en|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|WD=Q2517942|lt=ten stages of development|1=Bhūmi (Buddhism)}} that a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of {{ill|Buddha-nature|en|Buddha-nature|WD=Q640962|lt=Buddha-nature|1=Buddha-nature}} and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu|1=Vasubandhu}} in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by {{ill|Bodhiruci|en|Bodhiruci|WD=Q2324914|lt=Bodhiruci|1=Bodhiruci}} and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A {{ill|Dashabhumika|en|Dashabhumika|WD=Q155237|lt=Daśabhūmikā|1=Dashabhumika}} school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the {{ill|Huayan|en|Huayan|WD=Q1198075|lt=Huayan|1=Huayan}}, as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}.

{{Italic title}} {{MahayanaBuddhism}} {{short description|Sutra in Mahāyāna Buddhism}} The '''''Ten Stages Sutra''''' ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}: ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''; {{zh|t=十地經|s=十地经|p=shí dì jīng}}; {{bo|t=འཕགས་པ་ས་བཅུ་པའི་མདོ།}} {{bo|w=phags pa sa bcu pa'i mdo}}) also known as the '''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''', is an early, influential {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} scripture. The sutra also appears as the 26th chapter of the ''{{ill|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|en|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|WD=Q790258|lt={{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}}}''. Modern Buddhist studies scholars generally hold that these Mahayana sūtras first began to appear between the 1st century BCE and the 1st century CE. They continued being composed, compiled and edited until the decline of Buddhism in India.
==Contents== In the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the [[:en:Gautama|Buddha]] describes {{ill|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|en|Bhūmi (Buddhism)|WD=Q2517942|lt=ten stages of development|1=Bhūmi (Buddhism)}} that a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} must progress through in order to accomplish full Enlightenment and Buddhahood, as well as the subject of {{ill|Buddha-nature|en|Buddha-nature|WD=Q640962|lt=Buddha-nature|1=Buddha-nature}} and the awakening of the aspiration for Enlightenment.
==Commentary== There is a commentary which survives in Chinese called the ''Daśabhūmikavibhāṣā'', it is attributed to Nagarjuna.
Another commentary on the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'', the ''Dasabhūmikabhāsya'', was written by {{ill|Vasubandhu|en|Vasubandhu|WD=Q316343|lt=Vasubandhu|1=Vasubandhu}} in Sanskrit and translated into Chinese by {{ill|Bodhiruci|en|Bodhiruci|WD=Q2324914|lt=Bodhiruci|1=Bodhiruci}} and others during the 6th century CE.
==Chinese Daśabhūmikā school== A {{ill|Dashabhumika|en|Dashabhumika|WD=Q155237|lt=Daśabhūmikā|1=Dashabhumika}} school said to have existed in China at one time, which centered on this sutra, but was later absorbed by the {{ill|Huayan|en|Huayan|WD=Q1198075|lt=Huayan|1=Huayan}}, as the Huayan school's principal sutra, the ''{{IAST|Avataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', already contains the ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra''. The ''Daśabhūmika Sūtra'' can also be found in modified form in the thirty-ninth chapter as part of the journey of the bodhisattva Sudhana. The Huayan school declined in China after the death of its fifth and best known patriarch, [[:en:Zongmi|Zongmi]] (780–841), but they provided major foundational teachings for the Mahayana schools which exist today, such as {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}.
==See also== * {{ill|Chinese Buddhism|en|Chinese Buddhism|WD=Q847618|lt=Chinese Buddhism|1=Chinese Buddhism}}
